hints.where: 'Ubi' can mean 'where', but with a perfect verb it means 'when'
hints.how: 'How' would be 'quomodo' — 'ubi' here is temporal, meaning 'when'

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'is' — 'eius' is genitive singular
hints.dative: Dative would be 'ei' — 'eius' with -ius is genitive singular
hints.accusative: Accusative would be 'eum' — 'eius' is genitive singular

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'adventus' — 'adventu' is ablative after the preposition 'de'
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'adventus' (same as nom.) — 'adventu' with -u is ablative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'adventui' — 'adventu' is ablative, governed by 'de'

hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'adventus' with gen. -ūs is 4th declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'adventus' with gen. -ūs is 4th declension
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'adventus' with gen. -ūs is 4th declension

hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'Helvetiorum' — 'Helvetii' is nominative plural
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'Helvetiis' — 'Helvetii' is nominative, the subject
hints.vocative: Caesar is narrating, not addressing the Helvetii

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive plural in -arum — 'Helvetiorum' with -orum is 2nd declension
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive plural in -um/-ium — 'Helvetiorum' is 2nd declension
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive plural in -uum — 'Helvetiorum' is 2nd declension

hints.positive nominative plural: Positive nominative plural would be 'certi' — 'certiores' with -iores is comparative
hints.comparative genitive singular: Comparative genitive singular would be 'certioris' — 'certiores' is accusative plural

steps.reveal: facti = having been made (nom. masc. pl.)

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'facientes' — 'facti' with -ti is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'faciendi' — 'facti' is perfect passive participle
hints.perfect active indicative: 'Facti' is a participle agreeing with 'Helvetii', not a finite verb

steps.prompt: The infinitive of 'facti sunt' translates to?

hints.to carry: That would be 'ferre' — 'facti sunt' comes from 'facere' (to make/do)
hints.to speak: That would be 'fari' — 'facti sunt' comes from 'facere' (to make/do)
hints.to become: That would be 'fieri' — while related, 'facere' means 'to make/do'

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive would use 'fiunt' — 'facti sunt' with participle + 'sunt' is perfect passive
hints.pluperfect passive indicative: Pluperfect passive would be 'facti erant' — 'facti sunt' is perfect
hints.perfect passive subjunctive: Perfect passive subjunctive would be 'facti sint' — 'facti sunt' with indicative 'sunt' is indicative

hints.fero, ferre, tuli, latum: These are the parts of 'ferre' (to carry/bear)
hints.for, fari, fatus sum: These are the parts of 'fari' (to speak)
hints.fugio, fugere, fugi, fugitum: These are the parts of 'fugere' (to flee)

translation: When the Helvetii were informed about his arrival
